---
title: "5 Years of Freelancing in Europe"
date: 2026-04-25
date-modified: 2026-04-25
categories:
    - personal
---

I'm a freelance AI engineer living in Spain. I started freelancing more than five years ago. Here's my honest recap of how it's gone: how I got in, how much I make, how I find clients, and what I'd do differently.

## How I got into freelancing

In 2019 I joined a platform that promised you a better-paying job in exchange for a cut of your future earnings. The way it worked was that they'd put you in touch with
someone already doing the job you wanted, and that person would help you get there. In practice, I barely got any help from them, but it ended up opening my eyes to the freelancing world.  

They did suggest a few introductions. The most interesting one for me was with a freelance data scientist. In one of our first conversations, he mentioned almost in passing that he was charging over €500 a day. At the time, that was mind-blowing to me. He was doing roughly the same work I was doing, and making so much more money. I asked him how to find a freelance contract, and he told me to more or less spam people on LinkedIn.

So that's what I did. I set up a bot that messaged pretty much everyone involved in Data Science in Europe. DS managers, recruiters, the intern getting coffee. Most people ignored me. A few wrote back to ask whether what I was doing was even legal. Most recruiters dismissed me because I had no prior freelancing experience to point to.

![Me spamming people on LinkedIn](images/5-years-freelancing-in-Europe/linkedin-spam.png)

Not surprisingly, the approach didn't bear fruit. Over time, I realized my mentor wasn't really committed to helping. His plan to help me was nothing more than me randomly reaching out to people on LinkedIn with a bot. If I landed a job, he'd get a pretty nice bonus. But if I got my account blocked, the downside was all on me. So we parted ways. But the idea that I could make a lot more money doing the same work, without leaving Spain, stuck with me.

Two years later, in 2021, the freelance market in Europe exploded. A friend at the European Commission put me in touch with a recruiter, and I quickly got an offer. I also got into multiple hiring processes for contractors around the same time. My first gig was with the European Commission, and shortly after I switched to Deliveroo. And the rest is history!

## What kind of work I do

Over the last five years I've worked on a variety of things. Early on, I built clustering algorithms to identify trends across European research projects and the NLP layer of a public-sector knowledge database. I then spent close to two years building data pipelines that processed millions of data points a day to track competitors, and improving the ML models that powered them.

Since 2023, most of my work has shifted to LLMs and AI products. I've built AI workflows and agents for a range of B2B clients – revamping existing products to incorporate AI, helping startups ship AI products, supporting academic research groups working on AI, or teaching courses on these topics. I've also led the technical side of personalization at a global organization, where I've shipped a range of AI workflows. On top of that, I've been involved in experimentation work for several clients.

I'm an AI engineer / data scientist / data engineer / backend engineer hybrid, with some product management skills on top. For better or worse, I'm a generalist. 

## The good and bad of freelancing 

I wouldn't trade the experience I've gained from freelancing for a full-time job. But it has had its rough patches.

**The good parts**

The biggest upside is freedom. I work fully remotely from home, skip most of the useless corporate meetings, and don't sit through 360 feedback cycles with managers. Clients mostly care that I deliver value. I've always thought that most of the 360 stuff, the recurrent "how are you feeling?" meetings, and similar things are mostly performative. So I'm happy I don't need to attend them.  

The second upside is variety. I get to wear many different hats, sometimes within the same week. On one project I'm acting as a PM, figuring out how to scope and build a specific product. On another I'm a data scientist, planning and analyzing experiments. On another I'm an AI engineer, shipping features for workflows or agents. The intellectual range of the work has been one of the most enjoyable things about freelancing. 

The third upside is the room it has given me to work on my own things. Because I could work intensely for a few months, save enough to coast for the rest of the year, and then step away, I had real time to try out business ideas and side projects. That's how I ended up launching several ideas: Namemancer, [AItheneum](https://aitheneum.iwanalabs.com/), [Kasipa](https://kasipa.com/), and Lystinct. None of them worked out, but I got a chance to try them out! 

**The downsides**

The first downside is uncertainty. Compared to full-time employees, I'm always operating with shorter horizons. Projects typically run three to six months, and I rarely know in advance whether a contract will be extended — or whether I even want to stay past it. That forces me into a permanent "always be selling" mode, where part of my brain is constantly scanning for the next gig even while I'm fully engaged with the current one. It gets tiring in a way that took me a while to recognize.

The second downside — and this one is on me — is that I never specialized nor found a way to position myself so as not to be just another freelancer. My rates did grow over the years, but I've hit a ceiling I cannot push past. Clients hire me because I'm a "get-things-done" type of guy, but not because I'm *the* search guy, or *the* recommendation engines guy, or *the* AI agents guy. Being a strong generalist helps you get a foot in the door, but I left real growth on the table by never going too deep into something. 

## How much money I've made

I won't go into much detail here, but I'll share a rough idea of how much you can make in this space. 

My first freelance contract paid €350 a day. From there, rates grew quickly. For the last few years, I've been working at rates usually between €600 and €900 a day. On very short engagements, I often double my rates. In the US or parts of Northern Europe, you can definitely do better. 

I've also experimented with switching from day rates to deliverable-based pricing on certain projects, and I generally prefer it. When the contract is structured around an outcome rather than time, my rate effectively goes up, and my incentives and the client's incentives line up. The client doesn't care much about how many hours I'm putting in. They care about what they get. The only downside is that you can often end up in tricky situations if you're not sure you can deliver what you promised within a reasonable amount of time. 

![Revenue over time](images/5-years-freelancing-in-Europe/revenue-over-time.png)

Overall, I'm happy with how this has gone, especially in a country like Spain, where these numbers go a long way. But I also think there's real margin to grow. As I mentioned earlier, specialization and a better positioning could have helped me charge a lot more.

## How I find gigs

These days, I have a decent number of recruiters and past clients that stay in touch for projects. But it took a few years and a few different channels to get here.

In the early days, a lot of it came from being active on LinkedIn. Some of my posts got real traction. For example, [Ask Seneca](https://seneca.dylancastillo.co/), a small demo that showcased a simple RAG system in the early days of LLMs, did pretty well. It ended up on the front page of Hacker News and The Economist. Other projects have had similar results. That visibility translated into people reaching out to me about work.

Another big channel has been personal relationships. Friends from [Entrepreneur First](https://dylancastillo.co/posts/my-entrepreneur-first-experience.html) or previous companies I've worked with have reached out when they had projects I'd be a good fit for. Even old employers have reached out from time to time with contract work.

What I don't do much of these days — and probably should — is active content marketing. I've gotten a bit lazy on that front because I'm rarely out of projects. But I do feel I'm missing out on opportunities and potentially higher rates by not putting more content out there. 

## What's next 

I've never felt this uncertain about the future, for reasons both good and bad.

On the optimistic side, things are changing for me. By the end of this year, I hope it'll be more than just me at [Iwana Labs](https://iwanalabs.com). I've previously sold projects where I collaborated with other freelancers, but never this regularly. We already have a few projects in the pipeline, and hope to keep closing new ones throughout the year. I have a few areas I'd like to explore that would allow us to specialize and better position ourselves. But it'll take some time until we get there. 

On the scary side, I genuinely don't know what's going to happen to the software industry. The changes from AI have been amazing to watch. But my best guess is that we're heading toward a much more unequal distribution of the profits. I worry that the economics are shifting from the top 20% capturing 80% of the value to the top 1% capturing 99% of it. We've already seen the [$1B one-man company](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/02/technology/ai-billion-dollar-company-medvi.html?unlocked_article_code=1.X1A.fHR4.4vekZY08eLvB&smid=url-share) and companies with [crazy growth rates](https://sacra.com/research/cursor-at-100m-arr/). High margins and [ZIRP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zero_interest-rate_policy) have let the software industry bloat for years. A small number of companies will be able to keep those margins. But for the vast majority, margins will go down, and it's going to be a free-for-all unless you really have something special to offer.

And honestly, I have to ask myself whether I'll make it into that 1%. I'm optimistic about my skills, but the real world doesn't care about that. There are tons of amazing, highly motivated people out there. We're in an industry with virtually no barriers to entry, where the money has been good so far, and where AI can do a lot of the work. What was already an extremely competitive industry just got exponentially more so.

The freelance market is much tighter now than when I started. There are [layoffs](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/crm1y89vek8o) happening regularly. [Everyone can build now](https://www.washingtonpost.com/technology/interactive/2026/claude-code-cowork-build-apps/), and even if it's still far from perfect and can easily [delete your prod db](https://x.com/lifeof_jer/status/2048103471019434248), today's AI is the worst it will ever be.  

So the next chapter is partly about responding to all of that. Finding the right people to grow with, and figuring out where we can plant a flag. Five years in, I love what freelancing has taught me and the opportunities it has given me: the freedom, the variety, and the time to build my own things. But the next five years will need to look different from the last five, if I want to come out the other side of what's coming.
